text: Your PR will be approved without comments. Savor this omen.
A stakeholder approaches with 'just a quick question.'
The data you seek is in a CSV. The CSV is in an email. The email is from 2019.
A merge conflict in your future -- but also, a merge conflict in your past.
The sprint board reveals: you were the blocker all along.
Today's standup will end on time. You will be suspicious.
Mercury is in retrograde. So is your staging environment.
You will receive a compliment on your DAX. Accept it graciously.
Beware the 4pm Friday deploy.
The pipeline will run green today. Screenshot it for proof.
A wild LEFT JOIN appears. It is surprisingly effective.
Someone will say 'it works on my machine.' That someone is you.
The schema migration you fear will go smoothly. The one you don't fear won't.
A ticket marked 'low priority' in 2023 will resurface with vengeance.
You will discover a table with no primary key. Breathe.
The vendor's documentation is a riddle wrapped in a PDF.
Your dashboard will load on the first try during the demo. The stars align.
An intern will ask a question that reshapes your entire data model.
Today you will write a query so elegant it brings a tear to your eye.
The SLA gods are watching. They are mostly pleased.
A stored procedure from the before-times holds the answer you seek.
You will explain the difference between a fact and a dimension table. Again.
The build is hanging. It is meditating. Give it space.
Your end user will find the one edge case you didn't test.
A cross-team dependency will unblock itself. Do not question this miracle.
Someone will paste a full stack trace into Teams with no context. Patience.
The data is not wrong. Your expectations are.
You will be asked to make a chart 'pop.' The chart cannot pop. But you will try.
Today's Jira ticket will spawn three child tickets. The wheel turns.
A null where you expected a value. A value where you expected a null. Balance.
The VPN will hold steady through your entire demo. A rare blessing.
You will find a comment in the code that simply reads 'TODO: fix this.' It is from you.
The stakeholder will change requirements mid-sprint. The cards foresaw this.
A production incident will resolve itself before you finish writing the postmortem.
Your carefully indexed table will be queried with SELECT *. Let it go.
Your support ticket will be answered by a bot that has never felt pain.
The go-live checklist has one unchecked box. Nobody remembers what it was for.
The data is correct. The expectation is not.
If it hasn't hit the backlog, its folklore.
Your numbers are right. Someone else's aren't.
They want it exactly the same. But different.
The end goal was clear. To everyone except everyone.
We've always used this report. Why? Because our great ancestors did.
The requirement is final. Pending one more change.
That's a great idea for a future sprint.
A foreign key will be missing. The relationship was, in hindsight, casual.
You will know the problem is resolved when the user suddenly never replies to you again - keep onward
Today’s forecast: partly cloudy with a 60 percent chance of new data requirements.
A stakeholder will say ‘quick question’ and actually mean it.
Progress has been made, even if it doesn't feel like it.
Your story is almost done. Almost counts in testing.
